narrative:
Al Green has represented Houston's 9th District since 2005, making him one of the longest-serving members of the Texas delegation. In that time, he has carved out a niche as Congress's most persistent impeachment enthusiast — filing articles against Donald Trump three separate times in 2025 alone, each one swatted down like a fly at a barbecue. Say what you will about the man's legislative batting average, but nobody can question his commitment to the bit.

Green's real claim to fame in recent years has been getting physically removed from presidential addresses. In March 2025, he interrupted Trump's joint session speech by pointing his cane at the dais and shouting about Medicaid cuts, earning an escort from the sergeant-at-arms and a censure vote the next day — only the 28th in House history. Not to be outdone by his own record, Green returned to the 2026 State of the Union with a protest sign and was removed again. He called it "good trouble." His colleagues might call it a pattern.

On the 80% Bill scorecard, Green's record is a study in selective ambition. He sits on the Financial Services Committee, where the banking, insurance, and real estate industries shower members with contributions like confetti at a parade. Green has held this seat for two decades, overseeing the very institutions that profit from the financial opacity the DISCLOSE Act and dark money bans aim to fix. Whether he has used that perch to champion transparency or simply to maintain a comfortable incumbency depends on which press releases you read versus which bills he has cosponsored.

Green has generally supported Medicare drug price negotiation and Social Security expansion — positions that cost nothing in a district he wins by 50-plus points. On paid family leave, banning junk fees, and the right to repair, his record is conspicuously thin for a Democrat in one of the safest blue seats in Texas. On banning congressional stock trading, corporate PAC money, and lobbying by former members, Green has not emerged as a champion despite having the electoral safety margin to take on every powerful interest in Washington without breaking a sweat.

The uncomfortable truth about Al Green is that his energy flows almost entirely toward theatrical confrontation rather than legislative construction. Filing doomed impeachment articles makes for great cable news clips. Getting dragged out of the House chamber generates viral moments. But none of it moves the needle on the kitchen-table issues that 80 percent of Americans actually agree on. His district deserves a representative who channels that fearlessness into passing legislation, not just into getting escorted past the velvet rope.

Senator Kirsten Gillibrand has built a career on knowing exactly which side of a populist issue to stand on — and then standing there with a press release in each hand. To her credit, she has been one of the Senate's most persistent champions of banning congressional stock trading, having led the original STOCK Act to passage in 2012 and followed it with increasingly muscular sequels: the Ban Stock Trading for Government Officials Act with Josh Hawley in 2023 [1], and the Restore Trust in Congress Act in 2026 [2]. She has been beating this drum since before it was fashionable, which in Washington means she started roughly a decade before anyone else bothered to care.

On paid family leave, Gillibrand is the undisputed Senate godmother. She has introduced the FAMILY Act in virtually every Congress since 2013, proposing 12 weeks of partial income replacement funded by a small payroll contribution [3]. The bill has never passed, but Gillibrand keeps reintroducing it with the quiet determination of someone submitting the same screenplay to the same studio every pilot season.

Her transparency bona fides extend to the DISCLOSE Act, which would force organizations to reveal donors giving $10,000 or more during an election cycle [4], and the CLEAR Act targeting lobbyist-funded political ads. She voted to release the Epstein files and called on the DOJ Inspector General to investigate the handling of the case [5]. On Social Security, she introduced the Social Security Expansion Act to extend solvency through 2096 by lifting the payroll tax cap on earnings above $250,000 — making the ultra-wealthy pay the same rate as everyone else [6]. She supports Medicare drug price negotiation and has backed efforts to audit Pentagon spending.

Now for the asterisks. Before her 2018 corporate PAC pledge, Gillibrand was comfortably nestled in Wall Street's pocket. The securities and investment industry poured over $1.84 million into her 2018 cycle alone, with PACs from Deutsche Bank, UBS, and Bank of New York Mellon writing checks [7]. She ditched corporate PAC money when it became a prerequisite for the 2020 presidential primary — a conversion that was less Damascus Road and more campaign-trail GPS recalculation. She also voted for the $700 billion National Defense Authorization Act, making her anti-war rhetoric on Afghanistan somewhat complicated by her willingness to keep the Pentagon's budget well-fed [8].

Gillibrand sits on the Armed Services Committee, where she has been a relentless force on military sexual assault reform — a genuinely courageous fight against the brass. But on the 80% Bill's broader populist agenda, she represents a familiar Washington species: the Democrat who sponsors the right bills, gives the right speeches, and once cashed the right checks. Her legislative record on stock trading, paid leave, and transparency is among the strongest in the Senate. Whether it survives contact with the donor class she only recently divorced is the question voters should keep asking.

Brett Guthrie has represented Kentucky's 2nd Congressional District since 2009, a quiet stretch of bourbon country and Fort Knox where a Republican incumbent can coast to reelection without much fuss. A West Point graduate and former Army captain, Guthrie built a business career in the automotive parts industry before entering politics, and he has parlayed that background into a chairmanship of the House Energy and Commerce Committee — one of the most powerful perches in Congress, with jurisdiction over healthcare, telecommunications, and energy policy [1].

On the 80% Bill scorecard, Guthrie's record reads like a pharmaceutical lobby wish list. He voted against the Inflation Reduction Act, which capped insulin at $35 a month and gave Medicare the power to negotiate drug prices for the first time [2]. He then introduced legislation to weaken those negotiation provisions by extending the period certain drugs are exempt from price talks [3]. He has shown no interest in banning congressional stock trading, banning corporate PACs, the DISCLOSE Act, or any of the campaign finance reforms that command overwhelming public support. On term limits, dark money disclosure, and lobbying restrictions, his record is a wall of silence.

The money trail explains the silence. Guthrie set a House record in 2023-24 by raising roughly $530,000 from the pharmaceutical industry — more Big Pharma cash than any other sitting House Republican [4]. Over his career, that total exceeds $1.8 million from pharma and health products alone [4]. He pulled in another $221,000 from health services companies like UnitedHealth and Blue Cross Blue Shield in the same cycle [5]. After becoming Energy and Commerce chair, approximately 100 PACs that had never written him a check before suddenly discovered his campaign existed [6]. Meanwhile, Guthrie sits on the board of Trace Die Cast, a family aluminum-parts company in which he holds up to $10.5 million in equity and trusts — while simultaneously serving in the Congressional Aluminum Caucus and on a committee with jurisdiction over the auto industry [7]. Public Citizen has called it "a clear conflict of interest" [7].

The bottom line: Guthrie chairs the committee that oversees the industries that fund him, opposes the drug pricing reforms that 80% of Americans support, and maintains financial entanglements that would make an ethics lawyer reach for the antacids. Kentucky's 2nd District deserves to know whose interests their congressman is negotiating for — and the donor receipts suggest it is not theirs.

John Garamendi has been in elected office longer than most of his constituents have been alive. Born in 1945, raised on a cattle ranch in Calaveras County, he went from Peace Corps volunteer to state legislator at 27, became California's first elected Insurance Commissioner, served as Lieutenant Governor, and finally landed in Congress via a 2009 special election [1][2]. Now 80 years old and representing California's 8th District, he is a senior member of the House Armed Services Committee who still raises Angus cattle on his family ranch [3]. He is, in other words, exactly the kind of career public servant the 80% Bill was designed to test: someone who has had decades to either fight for popular reforms or find reasons not to.

On paper, Garamendi's alignment with the 80% Bill is impressively broad. He has been one of Congress's most persistent voices on repealing the post-9/11 Authorizations for Use of Military Force, introducing multiple bills over more than a decade to sunset the 2001 AUMF and force Congress to vote before sending troops anywhere [4][5]. He voted for the For the People Act (H.R. 1), the sweeping anti-corruption package that addresses gerrymandering, dark money, and voting rights [6]. He supported the DISCLOSE Act to unmask corporate political spending [7]. He voted for the Inflation Reduction Act, which finally gave Medicare the power to negotiate drug prices [8]. And he introduced the Fair COLA for Seniors Act to fix Social Security's cost-of-living formula so it reflects what retirees actually spend money on [9].

Where it gets more interesting is the Armed Services Committee, where Garamendi has spent his congressional career. He has pushed to rein in defense contractor price gouging, partnering with Elizabeth Warren on legislation to crack down on Pentagon overcharges [10]. He fought to restore $19.1 million in funding cuts to the Pentagon Inspector General's office and advanced provisions to eliminate waste on projects with egregious cost overruns [11]. Yet the defense sector is also one of his funding pipelines. Armed Services Committee members collectively received $5.8 million from the defense industry during the 2022 election cycle alone, and contractors like Northrop Grumman have poured $3.8 million into House and Senate Armed Services members since 2018 [12]. Garamendi has taken money from this ecosystem while simultaneously criticizing it, once quipping that his colleagues "want to buy more F-35s—that makes the companies very happy, but there's no money to maintain them" [10].

The financial picture is less dramatic than many of his colleagues. Garamendi's wealth is primarily in real estate—ranch property, rental property, and a cabin—rather than stock portfolios, with approximately $0 in publicly traded assets that trackers can monitor live [13][14]. His spouse sold between $1,001 and $15,000 in Twitter stock in January 2022, which is not exactly insider-trading territory [13]. For a man who has spent half a century in public life, the absence of a stock trading scandal is itself notable, though it also means a congressional stock trading ban would cost him nothing personally.

The bottom line on Garamendi is that he is a reliable vote for most of the 80% Bill's provisions—war powers, drug pricing, election reform, dark money disclosure—and a genuine advocate on some of them. The tension lives where it always does for Armed Services Democrats: you cannot spend your career on a committee that funnels three-quarters of a trillion dollars annually to defense contractors without some of that money flowing back your way. Garamendi manages this contradiction better than most, but it remains a contradiction.

Paul Gosar is a former dentist from Flagstaff who has represented various Arizona congressional districts since 2011 and currently holds the 9th district seat. He is perhaps the only member of Congress whose own siblings — six of nine — filmed a campaign ad endorsing his opponent, calling him an embarrassment to the family name [1]. He was censured by the House in 2021 for posting an animated video depicting himself killing a Democratic colleague, with only two Republicans voting to strip his committee assignments [2]. He has appeared at or promoted events organized by white nationalist Nick Fuentes, attended a gathering scheduled on Hitler's birthday hosted by the American Populist Union, and employed a staffer whom Fuentes called one of his "strongest soldiers" [3][4]. His own party leadership has publicly condemned these associations [5]. This is the man who would like you to believe he is a populist champion of the working class.

On the 80% Bill's provisions, Gosar's record is a fascinating study in selective populism. He voted to repeal the 2002 Iraq War AUMF, genuinely aligning with the end-forever-wars plank and stating that Congress has the constitutional duty to declare war [6]. He introduced a resolution requiring House leadership to wear body cameras — a cheeky transparency stunt aimed at Nancy Pelosi, not at police reform, but one that acknowledged body cameras "provide valuable evidence" [7]. He claims to oppose cuts to Social Security and has introduced legislation to eliminate taxes on Social Security benefits — though he was rated 0% by the National Committee to Preserve Social Security and Medicare [8]. On the Epstein files, Gosar once spelled out "Epstein didn't kill himself" in an acrostic across 23 tweets during Trump's impeachment trial, but when it came time to actually vote on subpoenaing the Justice Department for Epstein files in July 2025, he skipped the committee vote entirely [9][10].

That pattern — loud talk, quiet walk — defines Gosar's relationship with the rest of the 80% Bill. He voted against the Inflation Reduction Act and its Medicare drug price negotiation provisions [11]. He opposes taxing the ultra-wealthy, believing "individuals are best equipped to determine how to spend their own money" [12]. He has taken no meaningful action on banning congressional stock trading, closing the carried interest loophole, banning corporate PACs, passing paid family leave, enacting the DISCLOSE Act, or supporting right to repair. On Citizens United, he has said money and politics have always been linked and that "everyone has a chance to speak" — a position that conveniently ignores the difference between a retiree's $25 donation and a Super PAC's $25 million ad buy [13]. He has shown no interest in gerrymandering reform, and his 5.9% vote-missing rate — nearly triple the median for sitting representatives — suggests the populist fire burns brighter on social media than on the House floor [14].

The donor picture tells its own story. After January 6, Fortune 500 PACs froze contributions to electoral objectors like Gosar, and even the American Dental Association's PAC — his own profession — suspended funding for him [15]. But the mining industry, which gives over 90% of its contributions to Republicans, has been a steady supporter throughout his career in a state where extractive industries hold enormous sway [16]. The top corporate PAC donors to GOP objectors included AT&T, Comcast, Lockheed Martin, Northrop Grumman, and Raytheon, along with trade groups like the National Association of Realtors and the American Bankers Association [17]. For a self-styled anti-establishment rebel, Gosar's funding sources read like a Fortune 500 directory — or at least they did until even those companies decided he was too toxic to touch.

The bottom line on Gosar is that he is a performative populist who agrees with the 80% Bill exactly where it costs him nothing — tweeting about Epstein, voting to sunset a war authorization that had already lost its purpose — and vanishes precisely where it would require crossing corporate donors or party leadership. He is the political equivalent of a dentist who lectures you about flossing while selling candy in the lobby. Arizona's 9th district deserves a representative whose populism extends beyond Twitter threads and into the voting record.

Morgan Griffith has represented Virginia's 9th District since 2011, which means he has spent fifteen years serving the poorest, most medically underserved corner of the Commonwealth while chairing the very House subcommittee that oversees the pharmaceutical industry. If that sounds like a recipe for populist heroism, you have not been paying attention.

As Chairman of the Energy and Commerce Subcommittee on Health, and previously the Subcommittee on Oversight and Investigations, Griffith occupies one of the most powerful perches in Congress for holding Big Pharma accountable. He has used it to call Medicare drug price negotiation a "socialist pricing scheme" and to denounce the Inflation Reduction Act's prescription drug provisions as "so-called negotiation... not a true negotiation." His constituents in coal country — where opioid addiction rates are among the nation's highest and pharmacy deserts are spreading — might have a different view of what constitutes a fair price for insulin, but Griffith is busy protecting the pharmaceutical industry's right to charge whatever the market will bear.

The donor math tells the story. Health professionals are among Griffith's top contributing industries, funneling nearly $200,000 into his coffers in recent cycles. The energy sector — coal, oil, and gas — rounds out the portfolio. Coal companies give 95 percent of their campaign funds to Republicans nationally, and Griffith, who champions an "all of the above" energy strategy with coal as the centerpiece, is a reliable recipient. He has worked to fund reclamation of abandoned mine sites, which sounds noble until you realize it amounts to taxpayers cleaning up the mess left by the same industry filling his campaign account.

On the stock trading ban, Griffith offered perhaps the most revealing objection in Congress, worrying aloud about how a ban would affect his family's 20 percent stake in a community swimming pool. When the nation is debating whether lawmakers should be allowed to trade stocks based on insider knowledge, and your counterargument involves a swimming pool, you have already lost the plot.

Griffith voted against the Inflation Reduction Act, against the For the People Act, and joined the Texas v. Pennsylvania lawsuit asking the Supreme Court to throw out millions of votes in four states after the 2020 election. On the 80% Bill's twenty policies — from banning dark money to closing tax loopholes to term limits — Griffith is a reliable no, wrapped in the folksy cadence of a Southwest Virginia lawyer who has somehow convinced his district that protecting coal executives and drug companies is the same thing as fighting for the little guy.

Ruben Gallego is a combat Marine turned progressive senator who clawed his way from a Chicago floor he slept on as a kid to the United States Senate. A Harvard graduate who enlisted after 9/11, Gallego served with Lima Company, 3rd Battalion, 25th Marines in Iraq — a unit that earned the grim nickname "Lucky Lima" before suffering some of the war's highest American casualties [1]. He represented Arizona's 7th Congressional District from 2015 to 2025 before winning his Senate seat, and now sits on the Armed Services and Homeland Security committees. His biography is the kind that makes campaign consultants weep with joy: working-class Latino immigrant family, Ivy League, combat veteran, published author. The question, as always, is whether the resume matches the record.

On the substance of the 80% Bill, Gallego checks a remarkable number of boxes. He voted to advance the Ban Congressional Stock Trading Act through the Senate Homeland Security Committee [2], co-sponsored the COIN Act and End Crypto Corruption Act to stop officials from exploiting their positions for financial gain [3], and has been a leading voice on ending forever wars — telling Newsweek that "you can't really be out of Afghanistan unless you sunset the AUMF" and warning that without repeal, "the Pentagon can just always be resending troops back in" [4]. He introduced the Junk Fee Prevention Act, which passed the Republican-controlled House with bipartisan support and was later finalized into an FTC rule banning bait-and-switch pricing on hotels and event tickets [5]. He endorsed the FAMILY Act to establish paid family leave [6], backed legislation to extend Social Security and Medicare solvency by taxing the ultra-wealthy [7], voted for the Inflation Reduction Act's Medicare drug price negotiation provisions [8], co-sponsored the Democracy for All Amendment to overturn Citizens United [9], voted for the For the People Act to end gerrymandering and combat dark money [10], and became one of the Senate's loudest advocates for releasing the Epstein files — reading excerpts from a survivor's memoir on the Senate floor [11].

The follow-the-money chapter is where Gallego's reformer brand gets interesting. He has loudly criticized colleagues for cozying up to lobbyists — he hammered Senator Kyrsten Sinema relentlessly on the subject — while being married to Sydney Barron, a lobbyist for the National Association of Realtors [12]. Since 2019, Gallego has voted for 38 bills his wife lobbied on and sponsored or co-sponsored 17 more [12]. The NAR has contributed $34,000 to his campaigns [12]. In 2021, a corporate lobbying group called the U.S.-Qatar Business Council paid for a $22,000 trip to Qatar for Gallego and his wife, producing the now-infamous photo of the congressman riding shirtless on a camel in the Qatari desert on a lobbyist's dime [13]. It is, to put it gently, difficult to position yourself as the scourge of K Street when K Street is picking up your vacation tab.

Gallego is genuinely one of the more aligned members of Congress with what the 80% Bill represents — stock trading bans, ending forever wars, junk fee crackdowns, drug price negotiation, Epstein transparency. His combat service gives him rare credibility on military oversight, and his willingness to buck party orthodoxy (he was one of twelve Democrats who voted for the Laken Riley Act) suggests a senator who at least tries to follow constituents rather than caucus leadership [14]. But the lobbyist marriage, the Qatar junket, and the steady flow of industry cash create a gap between the populist rhetoric and the personal reality. The 80% Bill would find a co-sponsor in Gallego. Whether he would fight just as hard for the provisions that make his wife's employer uncomfortable is a question worth $22,000 and a camel ride.

Glenn Grothman is the congressman your political science professor warned you about — the one who says the quiet parts loud, votes against your interests, and then gets re-elected anyway because the district map looks like it was drawn by a gerrymandering enthusiast with a protractor. Representing Wisconsin's 6th District since 2015, Grothman sits on the Budget Committee, the Education and Workforce Committee, and the Oversight and Accountability Committee, where he chairs the Subcommittee on National Security. This means he is technically responsible for overseeing government spending — a job he performs with the enthusiasm of a fox auditing a henhouse.

Grothman has taken more than $1 million from PACs over his career, including over $130,000 from the insurance industry alone. His top legislative priorities — Education (22%), Taxation (18%), Health (13%) — read like a menu of industries that would prefer Congress keep its regulatory hands to itself. He has not co-sponsored legislation to ban congressional stock trading, end dark money, pass the DISCLOSE Act, ban corporate PACs, or enact term limits. On Medicare drug negotiation, he has sounded alarms about healthcare spending going up without mentioning that blocking price negotiations is precisely why it keeps going up.

But Grothman's real talent lies in his extracurricular commentary. He once called Kwanzaa a holiday that needs to be "slapped down once and for all." He labeled Planned Parenthood "the most racist organization in the nation." He introduced a bill to classify single parenthood as a contributing factor to child abuse. He complained that President Biden was not nominating enough straight white male judges. He was featured on HBO's Last Week Tonight in a segment literally titled "People Who Somehow Got Elected." These are not cherry-picked gaffes — this is the highlight reel.

On the Budget Committee, Grothman talks tough about fiscal responsibility while supporting tax cuts that balloon the deficit. He has pushed to vote on Social Security and Medicare separately from other spending — a move that sounds like accountability but is widely understood as a setup for cuts. His CRACKDOWN Act focuses on immigration enforcement, which is standard fare for a congressman whose district is 89% white and whose idea of diversity outreach is demanding Kwanzaa be abolished. In the context of the 80% Bill, Grothman opposes virtually every plank that would cost corporations money or empower ordinary people, making him less a representative of the people and more a cautionary tale about what happens when PAC money and culture war theatrics replace actual governance.

Vicente Gonzalez, the Democratic congressman from Texas's 34th district, would like you to know he's a man of the people — just don't ask which people, or where the money goes. The former attorney and self-described advocate for South Texas families has built a congressional career that blends populist rhetoric with a personal financial portfolio that would make a compliance officer wince.

Start with the STOCK Act, that toothless law meant to keep members of Congress from trading on insider knowledge. Gonzalez sold between $1,000 and $15,000 in Freeport-McMoRan mining stock in July 2021 and then waited nearly a year — 338 days, give or take — to disclose it, blowing past the 45-day reporting deadline by roughly seven months. He joined a bipartisan club of at least 65 members who've violated the STOCK Act, proving that when it comes to ignoring financial disclosure laws, Congress truly works across the aisle. The penalty? A potential $200 fine. Democracy is saved.

Then there's the creative accounting with campaign funds. Since 2018, Gonzalez has funneled roughly $33,000 in campaign cash to Tenant Services LLC — a property management company he owns and directs — for office rent. Members of Congress are technically allowed to rent from themselves at fair market rates, which is the kind of rule that sounds reasonable until you realize the person setting the rate and the person paying the rate are the same person. His campaign also paid $134,000 to Southern Texas Strategies for consulting services, a firm that raised eyebrows given its principal's legal troubles.

On the 80% Bill's priorities, Gonzalez is a study in selective engagement. He sits on the Financial Services and Foreign Affairs Committees — prime perches for tackling dark money, corporate PAC influence, and lobbying reform — yet has shown little appetite for using them. He hasn't championed the DISCLOSE Act, hasn't pushed for a congressional stock trading ban despite his own violations making a compelling case for one, and hasn't led on Pentagon audits or closing tax loopholes. For a Democrat representing one of the poorest congressional districts in America, where the median household income hovers around $40,000, the silence on taxing the ultra-wealthy and funding Social Security is conspicuous.

Gonzalez also claimed homestead exemptions on two separate properties for at least eight years, saving thousands in taxes that his own constituents — many of whom struggle to own even one home — were still paying. It's the kind of detail that writes its own punchline. South Texans deserve a representative whose financial disclosures arrive on time and whose self-dealing stays below the radar. Vicente Gonzalez can't seem to manage either.

Josh Gottheimer has represented New Jersey's 5th Congressional District since 2017, flipping the traditionally Republican-leaning northern New Jersey seat and holding it through multiple competitive cycles [1]. A former speechwriter for Bill Clinton, Gottheimer co-chairs the bipartisan Problem Solvers Caucus, a group of centrist Democrats and Republicans who position themselves as the adults in the room [2]. He sits on the Financial Services Committee, a perch that puts him at the intersection of Wall Street regulation and consumer protection [3].

On the 80% Bill's provisions, Gottheimer's record is a masterclass in triangulation. He cosponsored the TRUST Act on congressional stock trading but stopped short of supporting the more aggressive BAN CONFLICTED TRADING Act [4]. He voted for the Inflation Reduction Act, which included the initial round of Medicare drug price negotiation, but has been notably quiet on expanding negotiation to cover more drugs or closing the orphan drug loophole [5]. He supported the bipartisan infrastructure bill and has backed some anti-dark-money rhetoric, but has not cosponsored the DISCLOSE Act or the constitutional amendment to overturn Citizens United [6]. On paid family leave, he has supported the concept in public statements but has not cosponsored the FAMILY Act [7].

Where Gottheimer truly distinguishes himself is in the art of raising money while talking about reform. He is consistently one of the top fundraisers in the House, pulling in over $10 million in the 2024 cycle alone [8]. The securities and investment industry is his top donor sector, followed by real estate and insurance [9]. He has received substantial contributions from corporate PACs, including from financial services firms that his committee directly oversees [10]. His Problem Solvers Caucus branding gives him cover to vote against progressive priorities -- he was one of a handful of Democrats who initially blocked the Build Back Better Act to extract concessions for the bipartisan infrastructure bill [11].

On housing, Gottheimer has not cosponsored legislation to ban corporate purchases of single-family homes, a notable silence given that his district includes some of the most expensive housing markets in the country [12]. On the carried interest loophole, he was among the Democrats who quietly supported removing the carried interest provision from the Inflation Reduction Act before its final passage, a gift to the hedge fund managers who populate his donor rolls [13]. He has supported the SALT deduction cap repeal, which primarily benefits high-income earners in his district [14].

Gottheimer is the prototype of a modern centrist Democrat: progressive enough on social issues to keep the base, moderate enough on financial regulation to keep the donors, and savvy enough to wrap it all in bipartisan packaging. His record on the 80% Bill's provisions is not hostile -- it is carefully, strategically incomplete [15].
